explanation: The requirement likely means that whenever prop_0 holds, it must eventually become the case that prop_1 does not hold (prop_0 'leaves' prop_1). In LTL: whenever prop_0 holds, eventually not prop_1 must hold.

The requirement states that whenever 'prop_0' holds, it will eventually no longer hold until at least after 'prop_1' is no longer true. To capture the idea that 'prop_0' must 'leave' (i.e., stop holding) 'prop_1', we can write: Whenever 'prop_0' holds, it must eventually become false AND at some moment before or at that moment, 'prop_1' was true.

The statement means that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must become false (i.e., be left) at some point in the future. This can be formalized as: whenever prop_0 holds, it must eventually be the case that not prop_1 holds.

The requirement 'Any prop_0 shall leave the prop_1' suggests that whenever prop_0 and prop_1 both hold (i.e., at the same time), eventually they must not hold together in the future. Thus, whenever prop_0 & prop_1, it must eventually be the case that not (prop_0 & prop_1).

The statement means that whenever prop_0 is true, it must at some point later become false, i.e., eventually prop_0 is not true. Interpreting 'leave' as 'cease to hold', the global pattern is: if prop_0 holds now, it must eventually not hold.
This requirement states that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must not hold at the same time. In other words, in all states, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must be false.

This requirement suggests that whenever prop_0 holds, at some point in the future prop_1 must not hold (i.e., prop_0 must eventually result in leaving or absence of prop_1). In LTL, this is captured by stating: always, when prop_0 is true, eventually not prop_1 is true.

The specification says that whenever prop_0 holds, it should be followed by a state where prop_1 does not hold (i.e., prop_0 'leaves' prop_1). Therefore, globally, if prop_0 holds, then it must eventually be the case that not prop_1 holds.

This statement means that whenever prop_0 is true, it is eventually followed by a state where prop_1 is not true (i.e., prop_0 must 'leave' prop_1). One LTL way to express that 'every occurrence of prop_0 is eventually followed by not prop_1' is using the globally operator and the implication to an eventually statement.

The requirement specifies that whenever 'prop_0' holds, it must be followed at some point in the future by a state where 'prop_1' does not hold (i.e., 'prop_0' must eventually transition to a period when 'prop_1' is false). In LTL, this is expressed as: globally, if 'prop_0' holds, then eventually 'not prop_1' must hold.
